sentences.text: My name at birth?
Now, is Albert Roland.
R-O-L-A-N-D.
Rosenblatt, R-O-S-E-N-B-L-A-T-T.
No, not that I know of.
[LAUGHS]
My birth date is September 25, 1918.
It's 78.
I was born in Lodz, Poland.
Spelled L-O-D-Z.
Number 10 Zeglana, Lodz.
In an apartment.
We lived in the more roomy apartment than some people in our economical stage.
We lived in two rooms, two-room apartment, which was one was for bedroom and the dining room and the other one, a smaller one, was the kitchen.
On the first floor.
Yes.
Oh, there were about eight.
It was a suburb, which was called Balut.
It was an extension of the city which was Lodz.
It was just on the outskirts of the city.
Well, the neighborhood was mainly Jewish by nature, which was we had some Gentiles, too, but the majority was Jewish.
Most of the inhabitants were working-class people, poor people which were underprivileged people, if I may say so.
And the neighborhood didn't have the best reputation, but there were a lot of nice people, a mixture.
It depends which was overwhelming, the nice or the bad people.
But this is how the neighborhood got the name.
It was pretty much city-like.
Lodz didn't have any parks until at a later date they had a-- passed a law that they have to-- every landlord has to make a little garden in the backyard.
So because Lodz was a-- a city where there were a lot of factories and a lot of chimneys, some smoke stacks.
So we had a lot of smoke and a lot of pollution.
We didn't know the word at that time, what pollution means, but we knew how it looks, how it feels.
Well, the non-Jewish people in Poland, as a whole, was very odd, so to speak of.
Which we as children at that time, we didn't understand why there was no lack-- it was a lack of friendliness on the side of the Gentile people.
If I would go to school, I got beaten up on the way almost every day.
If I saw some Gentile kids, I had to go around the block, you know, in order to avoid to-- to come in touch with them.
So it wasn't an easy situation for Jewish people at the time, in general, in Poland.
I went to school-- you need the address?
I remember.
Ribnah 15.
It was a public school.
It was not like in the United States you build schools, you know, special buildings, you know?
With beautiful yards and playgrounds.
It was a rented, a private house-- I mean private building, where there were people, rentals, and the classrooms was mixed.
It was two or three stories were rentals, and the rest of them were where people were living, families were living in there.
No.
In Lodz-- in small places like villages, there were not enough Jews.
You know, they were mixed.
Where in Lodz, in bigger cities, they had segregated.
You know, the Jewish people had Jewish schools, which were public schools also.
The program of-- of learning was the same, except for religious hours that they were teaching was different.
Otherwise, everything was the same.
Not the religious training, but history, Jewish history.
Yeah, they taught the Jewish history.
We had special teachers that-- that taught Jewish history.
Well, my father's side, they were modern people, modern Jews, and they had a Jewish-- good Jewish educational background.
And but they were dressed modern.
From my mother's side, they were Eastern-- Eastern Jewish people.
You know, where they had different garbs, you know.
They were those black-- black long coats, you know, with beards and little-- little hats.
You know?
My grandfather, my zaide, was a kosher shochet and so was the rest of the family, were orthodox.
I was raised orthodox, but not strict orthodox.
In Europe, every Jew knew what's a holiday and we observed holidays, we observed the Sa-- the Sabbath.
And it was-- a Jew knew he was a Jew, you know?
And he knew where he came from.
No, no.
Because my father was from a modern family, they didn't wear no payos or nothing.
They used to shave and everything.
Yeah.
But they were religious, just like the rest of them.
At home we spoke Yiddish.
Polish-- see, the circumstances that we had, the relationship with the non-Jewish people, they made us feel like we were foreigners all the time.
So-- so Polish was like a foreign language to us.
Living in Poland, born in Poland, and our native language was Jewish, mother-- mother tongue.
And Polish was like a foreign language.
A lot of Jews didn't know hardly how to speak Polish, and they spoke with an accent.
It was in Polish.
Only religious school, like cheder, they spoke Yiddish.
I went to cheder a short time.
We couldn't afford to pay.
My father died.
I was very young, and he was 38 years old.
He died of TB, so we had hard times.
I was nine years old.
Well, my mom went to work in a factory, and that didn't last too long.
She got sick.
She had a slight stroke because of the big stress after her hus-- losing a husband.
With the three children she was left.
My older brother was 13 years old and I was only 9.
My middle brother was 11, so we were two years apart.
And my mother got sick after she worked a year or year and a half after my father died.
And then became very hard times.
We got a little bit from an uncle, you know, a little money.
And then my older brother went to work, and then my second brother went to work.
And it got there a little bit easier.
But still it was very, very not-- not a comfortable life economically.
Always was not enough food in the house, clothing was-- was a luxury.
You know, I had to wear clothes what my older brother passed to my next brother and my middle brother, then it came to me.
It was from pants and jackets and shoes.
Even though it was not my size, but this is the way it was.
I couldn't--
Well, we were too proud to go to agencies for help.
You know, I come from a proud family, so my mother wouldn't-- wouldn't stand for that, to go for any help to an agency.
My father's name was Bernard.
My mother's name was Tauba.
Her maiden name was Spiegel.
My older brother was Aaron.
My middle brother was Henry, or Hanoch, and mine was Abram -Avram.
Well, before my father died was not easy, because 10 years he was sick.
He was sick on TB, was a tuberculosis patient.
So during the war-- after the First World War, you know there was a typhoid epidemic, and he was sick, you know, because of that epidemic.
He was in the hospital, and when he recovered from the typhoid sickness, he got pneumonia.
And after a lot of people died from pneumonia, there was no cure, no help.
But if somebody survived, he got sick on TB.
So after 10 years, you know, being sick, it was not an easy life for him and for the rest of the family.
He was a traveling salesman, and that was very hard for TB patients, because TB patient had to eat on time and, you know, watch his, you know, way of life, you know?
And he couldn't observe all this.
And he didn't sleep enough.
You know how it is.
So after 10 years he was-- after less than 10 years, the last couple years he was mostly bedridden.
It was very hard.
And he wouldn't let my mom go to work because it was not right, you know, for a woman to work.
So we struggled really hard.
You know, it was not an easy life.
And did you go to-- when you finished public school, was that free?
Could you go to free school?
Public school was free.
But cheder--you had to pay.
Cheder, you had to pay.
That was private.
Well, to tell you the truth, there was not much fun.
The only fun thing is that we were children.
We were young.
You know, that was the only fun.
We were not able to think, you know, about the hard situation we were in.
We didn't realize it, because, well, we lived in a poor neighborhood.
So nobody was living a lot better than we did, and if somebody had more food on the table, in our estimation he was rich.
So this is the situation it was.
We lived in it.
I went to school to seventh grade.
That-- that was grammar school.
Then you had to go to middle school, which was private.
You had to pay, which we couldn't afford.
No, that was also private.
Just as well.
After-- after we finished school, we had to go learn a trade.
Not in school, but you had to learn a trade on the spot, you know?
They had private-- like the tailor's shop, you know?
Which the shop was the boss with one worker, you know?
And I was the third one, and they were supposed to teach me how to do it, how to hold the needle or whatever.
And most of time we had to clean the house or do other-- or take the kids for a walk, you know.
It was not exactly the way it should have been to teach a trade a boy, but they were exploiting, you know, us the best way they know how.
Well, this is what-- what I got the training from a private--
--person.
And until I knew how to do it, you know, first of all the tailor trade was seasonal, you know?
Then I went to-- to work as a learn, to be a furrier.
And until this came about, until we'd understood that it's a long-term thing and I wouldn't make any money until three years hence.
So I got a chance to get into a factory and learn a trade over there, which was in a shorter period of time.
In the meantime, I started earning a little bit of money, which was more or less decent, the war broke out.
The Second World War.
I was too young.
I was too young, yeah.
Well, we heard about what went on in Germany, how the situation of the German Jewish people was under Hitler.
And also there was the Kristallnacht, which our papers were writing, you know, what went on.
And then Hitler threw out Jewish people that were Polish citizens that were never became German citizens.
So he just brought them to the border.
He just was shooting in the air so they will run, scared, you know, across the bord-- the Polish border.
So and then they didn't have no place to go.
They-- a lot of them were born over there, because their parents were not citizens and they were not citizens.
They didn't even know the-- the Polish language.
And this is how we learned, you know, there's nothing, you know, good to-- to wait from the Nazis.
You know, there's nothing good that would come of them for the Jewish people in general.
Yes, yes.
I had an uncle, as a matter of fact.
He lived in Berlin, and he was one of those people that were chased-- that were chased out from Germany the same way.
But luckily, he had family.
You know, he had brothers and sisters, so he was not lo-- entirely lost, you know, in a strange country.
He went from Poland.
He was my father's brother.
He came from Poland to-- to Germany.
He lived many years in Germany.
And then when Hitler came to power, he was extricated from Germany.
His name was Emil Rosenblatt.
Well, we didn't know that this is going to take place in such a short time that the Germans will override Poland.
You know?

And we didn't give it a thought, because they kept writing in the papers that England-- England, which was a big power at that time, and France, you know, those two big powers, they will fight back, you know, in the case, you know, if war breaks out, and blah, blah, blah.
And so, you know, people are people.
They read the paper and they think this is what-- what is going to happen.

When war broke out, until we got to know, you know, what's happening in the 6th of September, 1939, they broadcast over the radio that all people that were of military age-- and I was already close to it, but my two brothers were already of military age-- they should take a little food, you know, and a little belongings and go out of the city.
What's going to happen there, nobody knew, but we thought, you know, maybe they're going to induct them into the army and they're going to fight back the Germans.
But in the meantime, what happened is that I-- I went with them.

And we left my mother, you know, alone, because my mother said, well, maybe it's better-- for the better, you know, that you go out and then you come back when everything was-- will quiet down, you know?
People thought that this is just a matter of a-- a few days.
So when we came out of the city, walking obviously, we didn't have nothing-- we didn't have no cars or horses-- we saw a terrible thing.
People were running, you know?
The military were-- were running, you know?
The Polish military?
The Polish military.
They were stepping back, you know, from the Germans, pushed them back.
And you had civil-- civilian people, you know, a mix-up.
And so they couldn't move fast enough, the military, with horses and cows.
You know, people were scared.
You know?
So they kept running.
And the Germans were bombing the-- the main routes, you know, where people were running.
You know?
They didn't care.
They were-- they lowered the airplanes.
They were shooting from machine guns and-- and dropping gre-- grenades and bombs, you know, on the cities.
You know?
They were bombing, you know, with-- with fire bombs.
And they set a lot of buildings afire.
It was a horrible, horrible thing.
When night-- when night came, you know, it was like hell.
You know, when they start shooting, you know, we ran into the field and we laid down on-- on the ground so maybe they wouldn't hit-- hit me or-- a lot of people didn't get up.
They got killed.
Then what happened, one of my brothers went far enough-- so because he went with a friend and he lived in a small town on the other side of the Vistula.
It was a-- oh, what was it?
How they say in English?
Like the Volga.
A river, the river Volga.
You know, so he thought that with him he was going to be safe.
And my other brother and myself, we-- we walked until-- because everybody said that-- that we have to go to Warsaw, and over there the Polish army will be able to push back the Germans.
So everybody was run-- was running in this direction.

But when we came closer to Warsaw, the Germans caught up with us, you know, and we figured there's no point in going any farther, because the Germans are already ahead of us, and they're going to be in Warsaw.
We heard that they're gonna put a ring around Warsaw, and there's going to be a big fight there.
So why should we be right in the middle?
So we waited overnight.
In the meantime, there was shooting between the Polish armies and the Germans, which the Polish wanted to go-- go through, you know, [INAUDIBLE] into Warsaw by-- by daylight.
We were in a ho-- in a house over night, by some people.
They told us to get out of town.
So we figured, my brother and I, where there to go?
Go farther east or go back home?
Because the Germans are here anyway, so there is no other place to go like home.
Because there's no better place like home.
But it was a great disappointment.
The closer we got to home, you know, they were looking for Jews.
That's all they had in mind, you know, Jews.
And they put us to clean up those bomb-- bombed home-- houses, you know, that they bombed.
And little by little, you know, they made our way-- we made our way through to the city, to Lodz.
And we got pretty scared, you know, because they were shooting people on the way.
And they were holding people, like in camps without giving them food.
So we tried to avoid the Germans.
We used to go through-- through-- through forest.
You know, we didn't go with the main-- the main streets.
And finally we made our way home.
And when we came home, it was a terrible thing.
You know, they passed all kinds of law-- laws against Jews.
The Jews are not supposed to walk on sidewalks, only on the pavement.
You know, and if you didn't, they beat you up.
And one of the days, you know, they came out with the-- it wasn't a law, but it was a-- how do you say it in English?
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Like an edict?
Huh?
Not a statement.
It's a-- they can shoot you on the spot.
What is that?
You had to put on the yellow band, you know?
To identify yourself.
You didn't have to identify yourself.
They already knew.
You know, they beat up people in the street, whoever wanted to.
You know, it was a horrible sight.
And one of the mornings, you know, we got up.
It was in the marketplace, they hung three people.
And I figured if the government can hang people in the streets, you know, nothing good you can expect from a government like that.
So that happened in a day where it used to be a holiday for the Polish people, like the 4th of July in the United States, when the Poles obtained freedom.
So my brother and I decided-- but because the third brother, we didn't know where he is, because-- What was his name, the other brother who left?
Aaron, the oldest brother.
Was Henry.
Mm-hm.
